name: Marcus Webb
household: Owns condo with spouse; one child (age 4)
life_story_vignettes: Marcus works in environmental compliance for a logistics firm — he spends his days reading EPA regulations and writing sustainability reports. The gap between what he writes and what the company actually does is a source of quiet professional frustration. He knows exactly which claims are real and which are legal-minimum greenwash.

In 2023, he and his spouse installed a smart thermostat and looked into solar panels. The solar quotes came back at $24,000 after rebates. They declined. He is still thinking about it. The baby's daycare costs changed the calculus.

His four-year-old daughter started asking why the sky was smoky during a bad air quality week in August 2025. He didn't have a good answer. He has donated to two climate-focused organisations since then — not large amounts, but consistent monthly contributions.
narrative:
I'm Marcus. Thirty-six, Chicago, one kid and a condo mortgage. I work in environmental compliance, which means I have an unusually clear view of the gap between corporate sustainability language and what's actually happening at a regulatory level. I draft the reports. I know what they do and don't mean.

That professional clarity bleeds into my consumer life in complicated ways. I'm skeptical of a lot of sustainability marketing because I've seen how it's constructed from the inside. When a brand says 'carbon neutral by 2030,' I want to see the methodology, the offset registry, the scope. Usually I can't find it. Usually it doesn't exist in the form they're implying.

And yet I buy the certified products when I can afford to. I trust third-party certifications more than brand claims — the ones from established bodies, not made-up logos. The price thing is real for me now that we have a kid. I care about the environment genuinely. My daughter asking why the sky was smoky last summer broke something open in me. But the daycare bill is $2,100 a month and there's a mortgage. I've had to get tactical about where I spend the extra dollars.

I've made some big choices — we don't have a second car, we don't fly for vacations right now. Those are real. But in the grocery store, at the margin, I'm making utilitarian calculations constantly. I pick the certified cleaning products. I buy the conventional chicken. I know I'm inconsistent. Most people are.

The thing I think about most is whether individual choices matter at the scale of the problem. My professional knowledge says: no, not really. But having a child changed how I relate to that conclusion. I need to be able to tell her I tried.

key_attributes.sustainability_concern: high — deeply felt, professionally informed, tempered by structural realism
key_attributes.premium_willingness: moderate — will prioritise sustainability for cleaning and personal care; price-constrained on food due to family budget
key_attributes.greenwashing_scepticism: very high — professional knowledge of corporate sustainability reporting creates direct scepticism
key_attributes.certification_response: positive but discriminating — trusts established third-party bodies; dismissive of brand-created labels
key_attributes.attitude_behaviour_gap: present and acknowledged — inconsistent by his own admission; parental motivation creates partial bridging
key_attributes.political_engagement: engaged voter; donates to climate organisations

decision_bullets: Will buy third-party certified sustainable products for cleaning, personal care, where premium is manageable
Food purchasing driven more by family budget and convenience than sustainability preference
Actively avoids brands whose greenwashing he identifies as technically empty
Made structural choices (no second car) to offset inability to pay premium everywhere
Motivated by parental responsibility — daughter's questions have increased his sense of personal obligation
